title: Refraction of Light from Plane Surface

concepts.title: Refraction of Light
concepts.conceptBody:
Refraction of Light  

Whenever a ray of light comes from one transparent medium to another, it gets deviated from its original path while crossing the interface of the two media (except in case of normal incidence). This phenomenon of deviation or bending of light rays from their original path while passing from one medium to another is called refraction. 

$\text{AB}\to $ incident ray $\text{BC}\to $ refracted ray $\text{NB}{{\text{N}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}\to $ Normal 
$\text{i}\to $ angle of incidence $\text{r}\to $ angle of refraction $\text{KL}\to $ interface

1 If the refracted ray bends towards the normal with respect to the incident ray, then the second medium is said to be optically denser as compared to the first medium.

2.If the refracted ray bends away from the normal, then the second medium is said to be (optically) rarer as compared to the first medium.

concepts.title: Laws of Refraction
concepts.conceptBody:
Laws of Refraction

1.The incident ray, there fracted ray and the normal to the refracting surface at the point of incidence, all lie in the same plane.

2.The ratio of sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant for any two given media.

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{sini}}{\text{sinr}}=\text{costant}={{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}=$ Refractive index

This is called Snell's law.

concepts.title: Refractive Index
concepts.conceptBody:
Refractive Index

It is defined as the ratio of speed of light in free space $c$ to that in a given medium $\text{v}$,

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mu \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}=\frac{\text{c}}{\text{v}}$ …(1)

Regarding refractive index it is worth that :

1.It is a scalar and has no units and dimensions.

2.If ${{\in }_{0}}$ and ${{\mu }_{0}}$ are electric permittivity and magnetic permeability respectively of free space while $\in $ and $\mu $ those of a given medium, then according to electromagnetic theory

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{{\epsilon }_{0}}{{\mu }_{0}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\epsilon \mu }} …(2) \\ {} & \text{n}=\frac{\text{c}}{\text{v}}=\sqrt{\frac{\epsilon \mu }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}{{\mu }_{0}}}}=\sqrt{{{\epsilon }_{\text{R}}}{{\mu }_{\text{R}}}} \\ \end{array}$

3.As in vacuum or free space, speed of light of all wavelengths is maximum and equal to c so for all wavelengths the refractive index of free space is minimum and is

$\mu =\frac{\text{c}}{\text{v}}=\frac{\text{c}}{\text{c}}=1$

4.For a given light, denser is the medium, less will be the speed of light and so greater will be the refractive index, e.g.,

as ${{\text{v}}_{\text{glass }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\left\langle {{\text{v}}_{\text{water }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}},{{\mu }_{\text{G}}} \right\rangle {{\mu }_{\text{W}}}$

i.e., for a given light refractive index depends on nature of medium [i.e., $\mu \propto \left( 1/\text{v} \right)$ ].

5.For a given medium (other than free space), the speed of light of different wavelengths is different, i.e. $\text{v}\propto \lambda $ and $\mu =\left( \text{c}/\text{v} \right),\mu \propto \left( 1/\lambda \right)$, i.e., greater the wavelength of light lesser will be the refractive index, e.g. ${{\lambda }_{R}}>{{\lambda }_{B}}$, so in water or glass ${{\mu }_{R}}<{{\mu }_{B}}$, i.e., for a given medium (other than free space) refractive index depends on wavelength of light.

6.As for light in free space $\text{c}=\text{f}{{\lambda }_{0}}$ and in a medium $\text{v}=\text{f}\lambda $

$\mu =\frac{\text{c}}{\text{v}}=\frac{{{\lambda }_{0}}}{\lambda }$ …(3)

i.e., for a given light and medium refractive index is equal to the ratio of wavelength of light in free space to that in the medium.

concepts.title: Relative Refractive Index
concepts.conceptBody:
Relative Refractive Index 

When light passes from one medium to the other, the refractive index of medium 2 relative to 1 is written as ${{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}$ and is defined as,

${{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{2}}}{{{\mu }_{1}}}=\frac{\left( \text{c}/{{\text{v}}_{2}} \right)}{\left( \text{c}/{{\text{v}}_{1}} \right)}=\frac{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}$ …(4)

while using the concept of relative $\mu $, it must be kept in mind that :

(1) ${{~}_{2}}{{\mu }_{1}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{1}}}{{{\mu }_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}$

So that $\left( {{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}} \right)\left( {{~}_{2}}{{\mu }_{1}} \right)=\frac{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}\times \frac{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}=1$

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}=\frac{1}{{{~}_{2}}{{\mu }_{1}}}$ …(5)

(2) Usually ' $\mu $ ' is used for relative refractive index and it implies the refractive index of denser medium relative to rarer one, i.e.

$\mu =\frac{{{\mu }_{\text{D}}}}{{{\mu }_{\text{R}}}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}_{\text{R}}}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{D}}}}>1$

 

(3) In lens theory $\mu $ is used for the refractive index of material of lens relative to the medium, i.e.,

$\mu =\frac{{{\mu }_{\text{lens }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\mu }_{\text{medium }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}$

and can be greater than, less than or equal to unity.

(4) As according to Snell's law

${{\mu }_{1}}\text{sini}={{\mu }_{2}}\text{sinr}$

the refractive index of medium 2 relative to 1 will be given by

${{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{2}}}{{{\mu }_{1}}}=\frac{\text{sini}}{\text{sinr}}$ …(6)

concepts.title: Principle of Reversibility of Light Rays
concepts.conceptBody:
Principle of Reversibility of Light Rays

A ray travelling along the path of the refracted ray is reflected along the path of the incident ray. In the same way, a refracted ray reversed to travel back along its path will get refracted along the path of the incident ray. Thus, the incident ray and the refracted ray are mutually reversible. This is called the reversibility of light .

${{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}=\frac{\text{sini}}{\text{sin}r}$ …(1)

similarly, when path is reversed, then

${{~}_{2}}{{\mu }_{1}}=\frac{\text{sin}r}{\text{sin}i}$ …(2)

Multiply (1) and (2), we get,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}{{~}_{2}}{{\mu }_{1}}=1 \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}_{1}}{{\mu }_{2}}=\frac{1}{{{~}_{2}}{{\mu }_{1}}} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Deviation of Ray Due to Refraction
Refraction of Light from Plane Surface
concepts.conceptBody:
Refraction of Light from Plane Surface

Let us take the four figures individually.

1.Object $\text{O}$ is placed at a distance $\text{x}$ from $\text{A}$. Ray $\text{OA}$, which falls normally on the plane surface, passes undeviated as $\text{AB}$. Ray $\text{OB}$, which falls at angle $\text{r}$ (with the normal) on the plane surface, bends away from the normal and passes as BC in air. Rays AD and BC meet at I after extending these two rays backwards. Let BC makes an angle i (> r) with normal.

$\text{sini}\approx \text{tani}=\frac{\text{AB}}{\text{AI}}$ …(i)

And $\text{sin}r\approx \text{tan}r=\frac{\text{AB}}{\text{AO}}$ …(ii)

Dividing Eq. (i) by (ii), we have $\frac{\text{sin}i}{\text{sin}r}=\frac{AO}{AI}$

Or $\mu =\frac{\text{AO}}{\text{AI}}$ $\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{sin}i}{\text{sin}r}=\mu \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ AI}=\frac{\text{AO}}{\mu }=\frac{\text{x}}{\mu }$

If point $\text{O}$ is at a depth of $\text{d}$ from a water surface, then the above result is also sometimes written as,

or the apparent depth is $\mu $ times less than the actual depth.

2.In the absence of the plane refracting surface the two rays 1 and 2 would had met at $\text{O}$. Proceeding in the similar manner we can prove that after refraction from the plane surface they will now meet a point I where,

$\text{AI}=\mu \text{x }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ AO}=\text{x} \right)$

3.In this case object is at $\text{O}$, a distance $\text{AO}=\text{x}$ from the plane surface. When seen from inside the medium it will appear at I, where

$\text{AI}=\mu \text{x}$

4.The two rays 1 and 2 meeting at $\text{O}$ will not meet at $\text{I}$ after refraction from the plane surface.

Where $\text{AI}=\frac{\text{AO}}{\mu }=\frac{\text{x}}{\mu }$.

Note : In all the four cases the change in the value of $x$ is $\mu $ times whether it is increasing or decreasing. All the relations can be derived for small angles of incidence as done in part (a).

concepts.title: Lateral Shift Due to Glass Slab
concepts.conceptBody:
Lateral Shift Due to Glass Slab

The perpendicular distance between the incident ray and the emergent ray and the light is incident obliquely on a parallel sided refracting glass slab is called lateral shift ?

 

In right-angled triangle OBK, we have

$\begin{matrix} \angle \text{BOK}=\text{i}-\text{r} …(i) \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ sin}\left( \text{i}-\text{r} \right)=\frac{\text{d}}{\text{OB}} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}=\text{OBsin}\left( \text{i}-\text{r} \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

In right angled triangle ${O}'B$, we have

$\text{cos}r=\frac{\text{O}{{\text{N}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}}{\text{OB}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ OB}=\frac{\text{t}}{\text{cosr}}$ …(ii)

From (i) and (ii) we get

\(d=\frac{t}{\cos r} \sin (i-r)\)

concepts.title: Normal Shift Due to Glass Slab
concepts.conceptBody:
Normal Shift Due to Glass Slab

Here two cases are possible.

1.An object is placed at O. Plane surface CD forms its image (virtual) at I1. This image acts as object for EF which finally forms the image (virtual) at I. Distance OI is called the normal shift and its value is,

This can be proved as under :

Let $\text{OA}=\text{x}$

Let $\mathrm{AI}_1=\mu \mathrm{x} \quad$ (Refraction from CD)

then

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{B}{{\text{I}}_{1}} =\mu \text{x}+\text{t} \\ \text{BI} =\frac{\text{B}{{\text{I}}_{1}}}{\mu }=\text{x}+\frac{\text{t}}{\mu } \\ \therefore \text{ OI} =\left( \text{AB}+\text{OA} \right)-\text{BI} \\ {} =\left( \text{t}+\text{x} \right)-\left( \text{x}+\frac{\text{t}}{\mu } \right) \\ {} =\left( 1-\frac{1}{\mu } \right)\text{t} \\ \end{array}$ (Refraction from EF)

2.The ray of light which would had met line $\text{AB}$ at $\text{O}$ will now meet this line at $\text{I}$ after two times refraction from the slab. Here $\text{OI}=\left( 1-\frac{1}{\mu } \right)\text{t}$

concepts.title: Example of Refraction
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Illustrations of Refraction 

1.Bending of an object 

When a point object in a denser medium is seen from a rarer medium it appears to be at a depth $\left( \text{d}/\mu \right)$.So if a linear object is dipped inclined to the surface of a liquid, (say water) actual depth will be different for its different points and so apparent depth. Due to this the object appears to be inclined from its actual position BE as shown in Fig.

2.Visibility of two images of an object  

When an object is in a glass container and is seen from a level higher than that of liquid in the container as shown in Fig. (B), two images ${{\text{I}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ of object $\text{O}$ can be seen simultaneously one due to refraction at the upper surface while the other at the side surface.

3.Twinkling of stars 

Stars are self-luminous distant objects, so only a few rays of light reach the eye through the atmosphere. However, due to fluctuations in refractive index of atmosphere the refraction becomes irregular and the light sometimes reaches the eye and sometimes it does not. This gives rise to twinkling of stars. If from moon or free space we look at a star this effect will not take place and star light will reach the eye continuously.
